design_philosophy.core_loop: Arrange dominoes on interconnected platforms, then push them in sequence to reach the exit. Each level presents spatial and logical constraints requiring precise domino placement and understanding of domino interaction types.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Puzzle-solving tension between spatial constraints and logical outcome. Player must visualize domino chain reactions before committing to the push, with failure meaning restart. Time pressure present but secondary—hints available after timeout.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Problem-solving mastery through observation and planning. The fantasy is being a strategic thinker who can visualize complex chain reactions and optimize solutions, embodied by the ant character navigating increasingly elaborate puzzle environments.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Pushover has a strict solution-oriented philosophy: dominoes are tools with fixed behaviors, not toys for experimentation. Each level typically has only one solution (though some allow multiple approaches). The game teaches constraint-satisfaction puzzle design where understanding domino types is prerequisite to success. Failure is immediate and frequent, enforcing analytical thinking over trial-and-error.

difficulty.lives_system: Single-life per level. Player character G.I. Ant can die by falling from heights, falling off screen, or being crushed by dominoes. Death triggers 'You Failed, You Died' message and level restart.
difficulty.continues_system: Password system allows resuming at any previously reached level without restarting from level 1. Token system provides single undo: after failing a level, one token restores the state to just before the domino push, allowing retry without full level reset.
difficulty.checkpointing: Checkpoint at level completion. No mid-level checkpoints. Password-based progress saving between sessions.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player has significant agency through token management (limited undo uses) and hint system (available after time limit expires, providing solution guidance). Puzzle solutions are deterministic; difficulty comes from understanding 11 domino types and spatial reasoning, not from action execution.

progression.unlock_mechanism: Sequential level unlock through completion. 100 levels organized into 9 themed worlds (11 levels per world + final bonus level 100). Each world completion awards a Quavers packet (SNES: cash bundle), serving as narrative progression markers. Level 100 requires solving dominoes with hidden markings, acting as final challenge gating.
replayability_hooks: Multiple solution paths exist for some levels, encouraging optimization-seeking replays
Token system encourages experimentation within failed attempts
Hint system provides teaching moments on puzzle design philosophy
Themed worlds create variety in visual and spatial design across 100 levels

world_building_techniques: Themed world progression: industrial complex → Aztec → space station → electronic → Greek temple → medieval castle → Meccano-inspired → dungeon → Japanese temple creates visual narrative arc
Character motivation (SNES version): Captain Rat drops cash down ant hill; G.I. Ant recovers it level by level
Collectible progression: retrieving Quavers packets (Amiga/Atari/DOS) or cash bundles (SNES) after each world marks narrative milestones
Hidden designer credits embedded in ROM reveal authorship (Helen Elcock, 1992) as meta-narrative artifact

environmental_storytelling: Industrial world establishes setting as mechanical/industrial
Progression through mythological (Aztec, Greek), sci-fi (space station), and historical (medieval, dungeon) worlds creates thematic journey
Japanese temple as final world suggests Eastern philosophical influence on puzzle design philosophy
Insect protagonist (G.I. Ant) in ant-hill setting creates coherent world-building through scale and environment
